sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 4 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, UP.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 91, level 2.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 2: you have used 4 of about 109 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The agent moved from rows 54-56, cols 36-38 to rows 52-53, cols 45-47. It's now inside the box at rows 50-53, cols 45-49. But notice:
- The agent's center is now "None" - it lost its center color!
- The agent shrank from 3x3 to 2x3 (rows 52-53, cols 45-47)
This suggests the agent merged with or was absorbed by the box. The level is not completed yet.
Looking at the image, I can see the agent is now at the bottom of the smaller box. The green appears to have changed shape.
Let me check the ascii to see what happened more precisely.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check the area around the box where the agent entered
ascii_lines = current_frame.ascii.split('\n')
print("Box area (rows 48-56, cols 43-52):")
for i in range(48, 57):
    print(f"{i:2d}: {ascii_lines[i][43:53]}")

# Also check all greens again
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAll N nodes:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}, pixels={n['pixels']}, children={n['children']}, hash={n['hash']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! Looking at the ascii around the box:
- Row 50: NNNNNwwccc - the box outline starts here (ccc)
- Row 51: wwWWWwwcww - there's a WWW (white) inside the box area
- Row 52: wwNWNwwcww - the agent (NWN) is here
- Row 53: wwNNNwwcww - more green below
- Row 54: wwwwwwwccc - box outline ends
Wait, the box was at rows 50-53, cols 45-49. But looking at the ascii:
- Row 50: cols 45-49 would be "NNNNN" (part of the larger green object at rows 45-50, cols 42-47)
- The box outline 'c' is at cols 48-50 in row 50
Actually, I think I misidentified the boxes. Let me re-examine. The 'c' characters form box outlines. Looking at the bottom-right quadrant:
- Box 1: rows 38-41, cols 45-52 (from earlier analysis)
- Box 2: rows 50-53, cols 45-49
But looking at the current ascii:
- Row 50: cols 48-50 are "ccc" 
- Row 51: col 48 is "c"
- Row 52: col 48 is "c"
- Row 53: col 48 is "c"
- Row 54: cols 48-50 are "ccc"
So the box is at rows 50-54, cols 48-50 (or similar). The agent entered this area and seems to have merged with something.
Wait, I see "NWN" at row 52, cols 45-47. The agent still has a W center! But the segmentation said center=None. Let me look more carefully.
Actually, looking at the segmentation output:
- rows 52-53, cols 45-47, pixels=5, children=[], hash=6c4307af74af2fba
This is a 2x3 object with 5 pixels and no children. But the ascii shows:
- Row 52: NWN (3 cells)
- Row 53: NNN (3 cells)
That's 6 cells, not 5. Maybe the W is a separate object now. Let me check.
Actually wait, the segmentation shows the green object has no children now. The W center might have been separated. Let me look at the W objects.
Hmm, but the level isn't completed. The agent is inside/near the box but something else needs to happen.
Looking at the image more carefully, I see the small green square is now at the bottom of the smaller box area. The box seems to have a charcoal outline. The green appears to be partially inside the box.
Let me think about what the goal might be. In level 1, the goal was to get both greens into their respective boxes. Here, maybe I need to:
1. Get the W-centered green into a box
2. Click (MOUSE) to swap centers
3. Get the next green into another box
4. Repeat
But the B-centered greens are large rectangles that might not fit in the boxes. Unless... the goal is different.
Wait, let me reconsider. Looking at the ascii again:
- Row 51: wwWWWwwcww - there are WWW (3 white cells) at cols 45-47
- Row 52: wwNWNwwcww - NWN at cols 45-47
So the green object at rows 52-53, cols 45-47 has a W center at row 52, col 46. But the segmentation says this object has no children. This is confusing.
Actually, I think the issue is that the green object changed shape when it entered the box area. The W center might now be part of the green object rather than a separate child.
Let me try a different approach. Let me see if clicking (MOUSE) does something now that the green is in the box.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check all W objects to understand the state better
seg = current_frame.segmentation
print("All W nodes:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'W':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}, pixels={n['pixels']}, children={n['children']}")

print("\nAll c nodes:")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'c':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        print(f"  rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}, pixels={n['pixels']}, children={n['children']}")

# Try clicking to see if it swaps centers like before
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 52, 'col': 46}])

seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
print("\nAfter click - All N nodes:")
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        rows = [p[0] for p in b]
        cols = [p[1] for p in b]
        center = None
        for child_id in n['children']:
            for cn in seg2['nodes']:
                if cn['id'] == child_id:
                    center = cn['color']
        print(f"  rows {min(rows)}-{max(rows)}, cols {min(cols)}-{max(cols)}, center={center}, children={n['children']}")

print("Level completed:", last_action_result.get('level_completed'))
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
